topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification blinked on my screen, a jarring intrusion into the quiet hum of my evening. My breath hitched, a sudden, shallow intake of air that felt too loud in the small apartment. It was him. Mark. The name sent a cold spike through my chest, instantly flooding my limbs with a frantic energy I couldn't control. I stared at the profile picture—a smirk that used to feel like a physical blow—and my hands started shaking so violently I had to grip the edge of the desk. Why now? After all this time, after I’d built a life that felt sturdy, he wanted back in. A wave of nausea washed over me, and I pressed my forehead against the cool glass of the window, wishing the whole room would just dissolve. I didn't know what to type, what to say, or even if I should type anything at all.

[story 2]

I reread the request three times, the little blue bubble mocking me with its casualness. Every nerve ending in my body seemed to be screaming. My palms were slick against my phone, and I could feel the heat rising in my neck. It was like being cornered in a hallway, the air thick and suffocating. I wanted to throw the device across the room, to shatter the glass, anything to make this connection vanish. My stomach twisted into a hard knot. I paced the worn rug, my footsteps heavy and uneven, the silence of the room amplifying the frantic thumping in my ears. I kept glancing at the door, half-expecting him to materialize, ready to demand an explanation I wasn't sure I could even formulate.

[story 3]

A slow, burning pressure built behind my eyes. I felt physically ill, a strange churning in my gut that made swallowing difficult. When I finally looked at the screen, the familiar shadow of Mark was there, and the world seemed to narrow down to that single point of contact. My fingers hovered over the 'Accept' button, trembling uncontrollably. I took a deep, ragged breath, trying to anchor myself, but the effort felt futile. The need to either ignore it completely or engage in some kind of agonizing exchange was overwhelming. I leaned back against the wall, closing my eyes tight, trying to force the frantic rhythm of my heart to slow down, but it only hammered harder against my ribs.
